paragraphs.text: Jacob continued his way, came to a well, and saw Rachel. She came with the flock, barefoot, in shabby clothing, and her face was burned by the sun. Jacob understood that He who had sent the beautiful Rebekah to the spring was sending the lowly Rachel to the well, and he shows her his strength: he rolled away the stone that covered the well, which many strong men could scarcely move. And having espoused Rachel to God by this wonderful deed, he himself was also betrothed to her by a kiss.

Jacob served for her seven years; but when the time was fulfilled, Laban deceived him and gave him Leah in place of Rachel. Laban resorted to such cunning not only because Leah was unsightly, and in seven years, while Rachel was a bride, no bridegroom was found for her, but also because he saw God’s blessing on his property during Jacob’s shepherding. So he plotted to make him a shepherd for another seven years, that in another seven years also his estate, gained in the first seven years when Jacob worked for the Leah given to him, might be multiplied. So Laban excuses himself before Jacob by the custom of his country and says: “It must not be so done in our place, to give the younger before the firstborn” (Gen 29:26). And then he reveals that this was indeed done by him deliberately, and says: “Fulfill her week” of marriage with Leah, “and we will give thee… Rachel for the service which thou shalt serve with me yet seven other years” (Gen 29:27). Laban gathered the inhabitants of the land, and they stood surety to Jacob for Laban. Jacob reasoned thus: if Leah remains in the house of the heathen Laban, the seed of the righteous one might there turn aside to heathenism. He also feared to lie to his wife Rachel, because a bride is already a wife. Therefore Jacob takes the one, that he may not break his word to her, and the other, that his seed may not become sinful through her. But if Laban had not taken away Rachel from Jacob and had commanded him to serve seven years for Leah, he would not have agreed to serve for her even seven days. And not because Leah was unsightly, but because it seemed repugnant to him to be the husband of two wives.
